title: Radar Analysis: Waveforms, Filtering, and RF Signal Processing Fundamentals
summary: This research paper provides a structured technical exploration of radar systems, signal waveforms, and filtering techniques from an electronic engineering perspective.

title: My Contributions to Vapor Quality Sensor Development (DoD R&D Program)
summary: Mixed-Signal PCB Design | Embedded STM32 Integration | Sensor Calibration | Hardware–Software Co-Development | Prototype-to-Production Transition
content:
Confidentiality Statement: All materials shown are non-proprietary, generalized, and representative of my engineering experience. Sensitive data, internal documentation, and protected technical details have been removed or modified to comply with confidentiality and non-disclosure obligations.

The following information is publicly disclosed in a published non-provisional patent, it is public record.




# Vapor Quality & Vapor Volume Fraction Sensor

**Named Inventor – U.S. Non-Provisional Patent (Public Record)**
Advanced Cooling Technologies (ACT)

While at ACT, I served as a named inventor on a vapor quality sensing system for two-phase flow measurement applications. The patent discloses the sensing methodology and system architecture; the summary below reflects publicly available information and my personal engineering contributions.

---

## Hardware Architecture & PCB Consolidation

The initial system consisted of multiple development boards and discrete sensing modules. I led the hardware consolidation effort to transition the prototype into a unified embedded platform.

My responsibilities included:

* Integrating multiple schematics into a cohesive system architecture
* Selecting and defining the four-layer PCB stack-up
* Designing power distribution and ground strategy for mixed-signal measurement stability
* Managing analog and digital domain separation
* Routing a production-ready four-layer PCB within enclosure constraints
* Generating and managing the complete bill of materials
* Coordinating PCB fabrication and component procurement

I independently handled revision control and hardware iteration management, tracking changes across multiple board versions as the system matured.

---

## Thermal & Reliability Considerations

Because the sensor operated in a demanding thermal environment, I incorporated thermal layout considerations into the PCB design, including:

* Component placement strategy for heat dissipation
* Trace width and copper area planning for current handling
* Mechanical integration within the enclosure to maintain thermal stability

These considerations were critical to ensuring reliable operation during extended test cycles.

---

## Prototyping & Manufacturing Workflow

I established and executed the in-house prototyping workflow, including:

* Solder paste selection (high-temperature and low-temperature profiles)
* Reflow oven configuration and validation
* Surface mount assembly and inspection
* Hardware bring-up and fault isolation

I assembled and validated multiple hardware revisions, iterating rapidly based on test findings.

---

## Testing, Calibration & Data Acquisition

I was heavily involved in experimental validation and data acquisition. My work included:

* Extensive hardware debugging and signal validation
* Calibration of the sensing system against external flow references
* Collection and organization of large experimental datasets
* Supporting interpretation of measurement outputs used in vapor quality calculation models

I worked closely with the software engineer responsible for implementing vapor quality computation algorithms. I assisted with STM32 firmware flashing, debugging, and hardware-software integration to ensure accurate signal interpretation and repeatable measurement output.

---

## Documentation & Technical Ownership

In addition to hardware development, I authored comprehensive technical documentation from scratch, including:

* Sensor hardware architecture descriptions
* Operational methodology
* Integration guidance
* Testing procedures and validation methodology

I operated with a high degree of independent technical ownership throughout development, particularly in hardware prototyping, revision management, and system validation.

---

## Engineering Growth

This work strengthened my expertise in:

* Mixed-signal PCB design
* Four-layer stack-up selection
* Embedded STM32 hardware integration
* Sensor calibration methodology
* Hardware-software co-development
* Transitioning research prototypes into manufacturable hardware

All proprietary performance metrics, internal documentation, and customer-specific implementation details have been intentionally excluded in accordance with confidentiality obligations.

title: Thermal Vacuum Materials Testing for Space Applications
summary: High-Vacuum Systems | Cryogenic Integration | PLC Automation | Safety-Critical Interlocks | Space Environment Simulation
content:
Confidentiality Statement: All materials shown are non-proprietary, generalized, and representative of my engineering experience. Sensitive data, internal documentation, and protected technical details have been removed or modified to comply with confidentiality and non-disclosure obligations.

# Thermal Vacuum & Cryogenic Test System Development

### Lightweight Space Materials Validation Platform

While at Advanced Cooling Technologies (ACT), I supported the development and validation of lightweight polymer-based materials intended for spaceborne electronics. The objective was to evaluate candidate radiation-shielding and thermally dissipative polymers under simulated space conditions, including high vacuum and extreme thermal cycling.

My primary responsibility was configuring, automating, and safeguarding a thermal vacuum (TVAC) test platform capable of long-duration unattended operation.

---

## System Overview

The test system simulated space-like environmental conditions using:

* High-vacuum pump staging
* Resistive heating elements
* Cryogenic cooling integration
* Automated thermal cycling
* Outgassing characterization

I was responsible for system configuration, safety architecture, automation logic, and experimental execution.

---

## High-Vacuum System Operation

I configured and maintained a thermal vacuum chamber to operate within the high-vacuum regime. This required:

* Pump staging and sequencing (roughing → high-vacuum transition)
* Chamber seal maintenance and O-ring replacement
* Helium leak detection and validation
* Monitoring across multiple vacuum sensing ranges

Through this work, I developed practical understanding of gas behavior in the transition to molecular flow and the operational considerations required for stable low-pressure environments.

---

## Thermal Cycling Automation

To evaluate material reliability, I implemented automated thermal cycling between extreme temperatures representative of space environments.

My contributions included:

* Programming PLC-based control logic
* Integrating relays and safety interlocks
* Coordinating heater activation and cryogenic cooling control
* Implementing thermal feedback loops
* Enabling long-duration automated cycling with shutdown protections

This allowed repeatable environmental stress testing without continuous manual supervision.

---

## Cryogenic & Thermal Integration

The chamber incorporated both resistive heating and cryogenic cooling to achieve wide temperature swings.

I:

* Brazed and validated copper cooling lines
* Integrated a vacuum-compatible cryogenic feedthrough
* Designed heater and cooling loop coordination logic
* Ensured proper venting and laboratory safety compliance

This required balancing thermal performance, vacuum compatibility, and operational safety.

---

## Pressure-Based Safety Control Architecture

To mitigate over-pressurization risk and protect both equipment and personnel, I designed and implemented a redundant pressure-triggered shutdown system.

Key elements included:

* Repurposing an industrial process controller to monitor vacuum sensor output
* Establishing a voltage-threshold-based shutdown condition tied to chamber pressure
* Automatically disabling PLC operation upon threshold breach
* Electrically isolating the cryogenic solenoid during unsafe pressure conditions
* Incorporating relay-based hard shutdown independent of software logic

This layered safety approach protected the turbo pump, prevented unintended cryogenic flow, and ensured fail-safe behavior during fault conditions.

In addition to electronic safeguards, I implemented passive safety measures including:

* Pressure relief integration
* Redundant thermal fuses on internal heating elements
* Heater current validation relative to feedthrough rating
* Grounding and electrical isolation verification

The complete safety architecture was reviewed and approved by the internal safety committee prior to continuous 24/7 chamber operation.

---

## Outgassing & Materials Qualification Testing

I conducted vacuum outgassing tests to characterize material stability under reduced pressure and elevated temperature conditions.

This involved:

* Controlled heating under vacuum
* Pre- and post-test mass measurements
* Data acquisition and logging
* Troubleshooting and refining test repeatability

I was responsible for running and monitoring test campaigns, analyzing trends, and improving system reliability across multiple iterations.

---

## Systems Ownership

This project required independent technical decision-making across multiple engineering domains, including:

* Vacuum physics
* Thermal system integration
* Cryogenic handling
* PLC automation
* Electrical safety
* Materials validation methodology

I operated with a high degree of ownership in system configuration, safety implementation, and test execution.

---

## Engineering Growth & Capabilities Developed

This experience significantly strengthened my expertise in:

* High-vacuum systems engineering
* Molecular flow behavior
* Cryogenic integration in vacuum environments
* Pressure-based interlock design
* Thermal runaway prevention
* PLC-controlled automated test platforms
* Safety-driven systems architecture

All proprietary material formulations, program-specific performance metrics, and internal documentation details have been intentionally excluded in accordance with confidentiality obligations.

---
